narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown determination in pursuing this path despite the challenges many face, and I want to help you understand the automated decision that led to your application's rejection in a clear way. The admissions model looks at patterns from thousands of past students to predict whether someone is likely to pass the bar exam on their first try, and unfortunately, it estimated an 87% chance that you would not—which is why your application was denied, as the school prioritizes those predicted to succeed right away. Your profile includes being in the lower third of your school's rankings for Year 3 grades, the third decile for Year 1, a middle family income bracket, an LSAT score of 30, an undergraduate GPA of 2.7, and planning to attend part-time while likely working. Compared to typical students, who average higher deciles around the 5th or 6th for both years, LSAT scores near 37, GPAs over 3.2, middle-to-higher incomes, and overwhelmingly choose full-time study (over 90% do), your situation stands out as riskier in the model's eyes. The biggest factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 decile ranking, as being in the bottom group strongly signaled lower bar exam readiness compared to peers who ranked much higher. Your part-time status came next, since most successful students go full-time and the model sees divided attention as a hurdle. Your Year 1 decile, LSAT score, and undergraduate GPA each added further concern, pulling the prediction toward failure because they fell short of the stronger averages that help others pass. Your family income had a minor role but didn't push strongly either way. While this feels discouraging, especially navigating these spaces as a Black woman, it's not the end—consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score, strengthening your recent grades through additional coursework, or exploring full-time options if possible to better align with successful patterns. You could also seek targeted bar prep resources or programs supporting underrepresented students to build your profile. Many have turned similar predictions around with focused effort, and reapplying after improvements could change the outcome. We're here to support your next steps.
